query: neonate jaundice clay colored stools liver biopsy giant cells

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Atresia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Biliary atresia is an obstructive cholangiopathy of unknown etiology involving both the intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts. It presents in the neonatal period with persistent jaundice, clay-colored stools, and hepatomegaly. It is fatal if left untreated with a reported survival of less than 10% at 3 years of age.

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Atresia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Children present in the neonatal period with persisting jaundice, clay-colored stools, and hepatomegaly. [17] Any child with jaundice of over 14 days should no longer be regarded as physiological jaundice and should undergo evaluation. More than half of the children with biliary atresia will have initial pigmented stools that later turn acholic and with the progression of the disease, signs of liver cirrhosis and failure set in with palpable hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, ascites, signs of portal hypertension, and failure to thrive.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Jaundice -- Evaluation -- Diagnostic Studies in Conjugated Hyperbilirubinemia
retrieved_docs.content: Radiology may be necessary to evaluate neonatal cholestasis. Hepatic ultrasonography can identify sludging in the biliary tree, gallstones, inspissated bile, and choledochal cysts. A triangular cord sign on hepatic ultrasound has high sensitivity and almost 100% specificity for biliary atresia. [81] Hepatobiliary scintigraphy is another modality utilized to evaluate neonatal cholestasis. Decreased excretion of the tracer 24 hours after introduction suggests obstruction and further differentiates obstructive and nonobstructive causes of cholestasis. [94] Prior treatment with phenobarbital improves the sensitivity of hepatobiliary scintigraphy. However, a liver biopsy is the gold standard for diagnosing neonatal cholestasis. Histopathologic interpretation by an experienced pathologist will identify the correct diagnosis in 90% to 95% of cases and may prevent unnecessary interventions in patients with intrahepatic cholestasis. [95]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Jaundice -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: A liver biopsy is usually necessary for a definitive diagnosis of cholestasis as it helps to differentiate biliary atresia from idiopathic neonatal hepatitis. Histopathologic features of BA include the expansion of the hepatic portal tracts with edema, fibrodysplasia, bile ductular proliferation, and the presence of bile plugs in the ductal lumen. Multinucleate giant cells and hemopoiesis are also findings noted on histopathologic examinations of cholestatic liver samples. [85] Although not diagnostic of any particular disorder, the prominence of hepatic erythropoiesis is seen more frequently in cholestasis of infectious etiology. Pathognomonic histologic findings of other cholestatic conditions include periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) positive granules noted with alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, a paucity of bile ducts associated with Alagille syndrome, and periductal necrosis and inflammation seen in sclerosing cholangitis. [86] Among familial causes of cholestasis, canalicular cholestasis with a marked absence of ductular proliferation and isolated periportal biliary metaplasia of hepatocytes is commonly seen in patients with PFIC type 1. Though the histopathology of PFIC type 2 is similar, the altered liver architecture and extensive lobular and portal fibrosis with inflammation occur more frequently. [53]

retrieved_docs.title: Jaundice -- Etiology -- Reduced hepatic uptake of bilirubin
retrieved_docs.content: Gilbert syndrome [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Atresia -- Evaluation -- Liver Biopsy
retrieved_docs.content: Liver biopsy can differentiate biliary atresia from other causes of cholestatic jaundice with a high level of accuracy. Features suggestive of biliary atresia include bile duct proliferation, bile plugging, multinucleated giant cells, focal necrosis of liver parenchyma, extramedullary hemopoiesis, and inflammatory cell infiltrate. Among these features, bile ductular proliferation is considered the most sensitive and specific feature. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Jaundice -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: A pediatric or neonatal clinician can manage most patients with unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia. However, patients suspected of having genetic causes of hyperbilirubinemia may need consultation and follow-up with a pediatric gastroenterologist, hematologist, and medical geneticist.

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Atresia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Other studies favor an acquired etiology with seasonal clustering of the cases especially in the winter months and also because 50% of the children with the disease have pigmented stools earlier in life which then later became clay-colored.

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Atresia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Biliary atresia is a disease of the intrahepatic or extrahepatic bile ducts with an unknown etiology. It presents in neonates with jaundice, clay-colored stool, and hepatomegaly. This activity illustrates the evaluation and management of biliary atresia and explains the role of the interprofessional team in improving care for patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Jaundice -- Evaluation -- Diagnostic Studies in Conjugated Hyperbilirubinemia
retrieved_docs.content: Serum aminotransferases may provide evidence of hepatocellular injury in patients with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia. Alkaline phosphatase and elevated GGT levels may indicate an obstruction in biliary channels. The prothrombin time (PT), the international normalized ratio (INR), and serum albumin evaluate hepatic synthesis and function. Additional tests like TORCH titers for in-utero infections, urine cultures, viral cultures, serologic titers, newborn screening, tests for inborn errors of metabolism, alpha-1 antitrypsin phenotype, and genetic profiles may be diagnostic.

retrieved_docs.title: Jaundice -- Etiology -- Defect of sinusoidal reuptake of conjugated bilirubin
retrieved_docs.content: Rotor syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Jaundice -- Etiology -- Conjugated Hyperbilirubinemia
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic : There are several genetic etiologies (eg, Alagille syndrome, alpha-1 anti-trypsin deficiency, galactosemia, fructosemia, Tyrosinemia type 1, cystic fibrosis, progressive familial intrahepatic cholestasis [PFIC], Aagenaes syndrome, Dubin-Johnson syndrome, bile acid synthesis disorders [BSAD]) that commonly result in CHB. Alagille syndrome (ALGS) is an AD disorder caused by mutations in the JAG1 or NOTCH2 genes, leading to a lack of interlobular bile ducts. [46] With an incidence of 1 in 30,000 live births, ALGS is the most common cause of familial intrahepatic cholestasis, though CHB in patients with ALGS may resolve with age. [47] [35] Characteristic clinical features include butterfly vertebrae, congenital heart defects (eg, peripheral pulmonic stenosis), kidney involvement, dysmorphic features (eg, broad forehead and a small pointed chin), and posterior embryotoxon of the eye. Gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT) levels are elevated, often up to 20 times the standard value. Occasionally, patients with cystic fibrosis (CF) present with cholestasis because of abnormal bile that plugs the bile ducts. [48] In developing nations where newborn screening with immunoreactive trypsinogen is unavailable, neonatal cholestasis may be the first clue to diagnosing CF. Alpha-1-antitrypsin deficiency is the most common genetic cause of cholestasis and can mimic biliary atresia in early infancy. Accumulation of anti-trypsin polymers in the hepatocyte endoplasmic reticulum of a patient with the PIZZ genotype leads to apoptosis of hepatocytes, ultimately resulting in cholestasis and cirrhosis later in childhood. [49] As with ALGS, cholestasis may improve with age. Galactosemia, fructosemia, and tyrosinemia type 1 are a few of the inborn errors of metabolism known to cause cholestasis in neonates. Newborns with galactosemia can present with cholestatic jaundice, cataracts, hepatomegaly, failure to thrive, renal tubular acidosis, and Escherichia coli sepsis after ingesting galactose from milk. [50] Galactose-1-phosphate uridyl transferase (GALT) deficiency leads to the accumulation of toxic galactose metabolites in multiple organs. The presence of urine-reducing substances suggests galactosemia, but GALT activity in the liver or erythrocytes confirms the diagnosis. Neonatal cholestasis is a presenting feature in hereditary tyrosinemia type 1, another AR disorder caused by a deficiency of the enzyme fumarylacetoacetate hydroxylase. Other characteristics of this disorder include renal Fanconi syndrome, hepatomegaly, coagulation abnormality, and the risk of hepatocellular carcinoma in untreated older patients. [51] Progressive familial intrahepatic cholestasis (PFIC) is a heterogeneous group of 3 genetic disorders involving canalicular hepatobiliary transport that presents with cholestasis. [52] Types 1 and 2 usually manifest in the neonatal period, while type 3 presents later in infancy. Affected patients with PFIC frequently develop cirrhosis and end-stage liver disease during childhood. In diagnostic studies, the GGT level is within the normal range in types 1 and 2 and elevated in type 3 patients. PFIC type 1 is caused by a mutation in the ATP8B1 gene, which encodes the FIC1 protein, whereas PFIC type 2 is caused by a mutation in the ABCB11 gene, which encodes for the bile salt excretory protein (BSEP). PFIC type 3 is caused by a mutation in the ABCB4 gene, which encodes for the multi-drug resistant-3 protein (MDR3). [53] Aagenaes syndrome, also known as lymphedema cholestasis syndrome (LCS), is another idiopathic familial intrahepatic cholestasis syndrome characterized by neonatal cholestasis and lymphedema in the lower extremities. Aagenaes syndrome is transmitted as an autosomal recessive (AR) trait usually seen in individuals of Norwegian descent. [54] Dubin-Johnson syndrome (DJS) is a rare AR disorder caused by a mutation in the ABCC2 gene, which codes for a non-biliary ion transporter in the liver. A unique feature of DJS is a black-colored liver and the excretion of coproporphyrin 1 in urine. [55] Bile acid synthesis disorder (BASD) results from a deficiency of an enzyme involved in synthesizing bile acids from cholesterol. BASDs are an uncommon cause of cholestasis, but many are curable with medical therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Cholestatic Jaundice  -- Evaluation -- Liver Biopsy
retrieved_docs.content: A liver biopsy can be helpful in cases of suspected intrahepatic cholestasis when there is diagnostic uncertainty.

retrieved_docs.title: Jaundice -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment of choice for jaundice is the correction of the underlying hepatobiliary or hematological disease, when possible.

retrieved_docs.title: Unconjugated Hyperbilirubinemia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 50% of neonates present with physiologic jaundice during the first five days after birth. Nonphysiologic, Lucey-Driscoll syndrome, or maternal serum jaundice in neonates results from maternal serum during the initial four days after delivery. [12] The onset of asymptomatic jaundice usually characterizes the overproduction of bilirubin due to ineffective erythropoiesis (early labeled bilirubin production). Patients with Gilbert syndrome are generally asymptomatic and present with mild or intermittent unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia without any liver disease or hemolysis, and on clinical examination may manifest jaundice. Some nonspecific symptoms, such as fatigue, abdominal cramps, and malaise, are commonly observed in this disease. Infants with Crigler-Najjar type I syndrome present jaundice in the first few days after birth with neurological sequelae if UCB is greater than 20 mg/dL. By the second week of an infant’s life, it rapidly progresses to life-threatening severe jaundice that requires treatment with exchange transfusion, intensive phototherapy, and liver transplantation. [28] However, patients with Crigler-Najjar type II syndrome, are mostly asymptomatic and may rarely present with jaundice and kernicterus. During the physical examination, the color of the skin and sclerae should be examined carefully under light for the evaluation of jaundice. For example, in patients with mild jaundice or hyperbilirubinemia (2 to 3 mg/dL), only scleral icterus can be seen. Also, with hemolytic anemia, there might be anemia and splenomegaly.

retrieved_docs.title: Jaundice -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The best initial step in the evaluation of jaundice is history and physical examination. The next step is to classify jaundice by etiology and type-Hepatocellular, cholestatic or mixed. The basic mechanisms of jaundice include elevated production, decreased uptake, and faulty conjugation. Treatment focuses on the cause.

retrieved_docs.content: outcomes, fetal growth disturbances, a spectrum of neonatal infections, genetic syndromes, bleeding events, various forms of jaundice, and traumatic injuries, providing a comprehensive framework for perinatal assessment, diagnosis, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2268
retrieved_docs.content: The neonatal qualifier value encompasses several newborn conditions. It includes umbilical bleeding after birth, which can occur when a loose umbilical cord clamp leads to bleeding. It also occurs in various forms of neonatal jaundice, such as breast‑milk jaundice, physiologic jaundice that results from immature liver function in fetuses or newborns, and jaundice that is linked to premature delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1081
retrieved_docs.content: NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN is a complex neonatal condition that gives rise to several related complications. One important manifestation is neonatal jaundice that results from delayed conjugation due to the immature development of the hepatic conjugating system. In addition, the condition includes نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage), which reflects the hemorrhagic component of the disease. Various forms of neonatal jaundice are also linked to this disorder: يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice) in general, يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (neonatal jaundice caused by hemolysis of the fetus or newborn), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (jaundice associated with preterm birth). Together, these interrelated entities illustrate how hemorrhagic and hemolytic processes in the newborn can impair liver function, lead to delayed bilirubin conjugation, and produce respiratory bleeding, underscoring the need for comprehensive assessment and coordinated management of affected infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1200
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn is linked to a spectrum of neonatal jaundice conditions and related complications. It is associated with neonatal jaundice caused by delayed conjugation, as well as breast‑milk jaundice, physiologic (newborn) jaundice, and jaundice that is specifically related to preterm birth. In addition, this condition can be accompanied by a loose umbilical cord clamp that leads to bleeding. Recognizing these interconnected manifestations is important for comprehensive assessment and management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 1163
retrieved_docs.content: The qualifier “Neonatal (qualifier value)” designates conditions that manifest during the newborn period. It is applied to the occurrence of نزف سري بعد الولادة (umbilical bleeding after birth), نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract bleeding), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), يرقان نووي (physiologic neonatal jaundice), and يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (physiologic jaundice resulting from immature hepatic conjugation in fetuses or newborns). Recognizing these entities as neonatal helps clinicians focus assessment and management on disorders specific to the early post‑natal timeframe.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2521
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is a neonatal condition that occurs in the neonatal period. The qualifier “Neonatal (qualifier value)” is linked to several specific forms of neonatal jaundice, including neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, neonatal jaundice due to delayed development of the conjugating system, as well as the Arabic‑described entities “يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد” (neonatal jaundice resulting from immature conjugation in the fetus or newborn) and “يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان” (neonatal jaundice associated with premature birth). All of these entities share the common relationship of occurring in the neonatal stage, highlighting the importance of recognizing various etiologies of jaundice and related disorders within this early life period for appropriate diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 937
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified as a specific form of NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN. The broader category of NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN encompasses several distinct types of neonatal jaundice, including Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, the general condition يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice), يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (neonatal jaundice caused by hemolysis in the fetus or newborn), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (neonatal jaundice associated with preterm birth). These interrelated entities reflect different pathophysiological mechanisms—such as hemolysis, immature hepatic conjugation capacity, and prematurity—that can contribute to hemorrhagic and hemolytic disease in the newborn. Recognizing how Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome fits within this spectrum and how the various forms of neonatal jaundice relate to the overarching disease category is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and targeted management of affected infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2429
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn is a complex neonatal condition that can give rise to several related complications. One important manifestation is neonatal jaundice caused by delayed conjugation due to the immature development of the hepatic conjugating system. The disorder may also be accompanied by post‑natal umbilical bleeding (نزف سري بعد الولادة) and bleeding from the neonatal respiratory tract (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ). In addition, the disease can produce a form of fetal‑origin jaundice (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد) and a jaundice that is specifically linked to premature birth (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان). Recognizing these child conditions is essential for comprehensive assessment and management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1138
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified as a neonatal condition, indicating that it manifests during the newborn period. The neonatal (qualifier value) stage is also the context in which several specific clinical entities occur, including نزف سري بعد الولادة (umbilical bleeding after birth), نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract bleeding), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (premature‑related neonatal jaundice). Recognizing that these entities all occur in the neonatal timeframe helps clinicians understand the spectrum of disorders and presentations that may be present in newborns, including those affected by Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome, and guides appropriate evaluation and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1199
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is identified as a neonatal condition, indicating that it manifests during the newborn period. The neonatal qualifier encompasses several distinct clinical entities. Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system represents a form of early‑life hyperbilirubinemia caused by the immature hepatic capacity to process bilirubin. In addition, neonatal hemorrhage after birth (نزف سري بعد الولادة) and neonatal respiratory tract bleeding (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ) are recognized as bleeding complications that can occur in the immediate post‑natal period, affecting either the umbilical region or the respiratory passages. Another neonatal manifestation is nuclear jaundice resulting from the protective coating (التمنيع الإسوي) in embryos or newborns (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد), which reflects a specific type of jaundice linked to fetal or neonatal physiological factors. Together, these entities illustrate the range of disorders and complications that are characteristic of the neonatal stage, emphasizing the importance of early recognition and management of metabolic, hemorrhagic, and respiratory conditions in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1206
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial sepsis of the newborn (unspecified), Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome, neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation (including the specific form caused by delayed development of the conjugating system), نزيف حديثي الولادة (neonatal hemorrhage), and يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice) are all conditions that occur during the neonatal period. The qualifier “Neonatal” therefore applies to each of these entities, indicating that their onset, presentation, or occurrence is confined to the first weeks of life. Recognizing that these diverse disorders share the common temporal context of the neonatal stage helps clinicians focus diagnostic evaluation and management strategies appropriate for newborn patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 917
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified under neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn. This broader category encompasses several specific neonatal conditions, including نزف سري بعد الولادة (umbilical hemorrhage after birth), نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (respiratory tract hemorrhage in the newborn), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), and يرقان نووي (physiologic neonatal jaundice). Together, these entities represent key hemorrhagic and hyperbilirubinemic presentations that are important for the diagnosis, monitoring, and management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2676
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the liver and intrahepatic bile duct manifests as سرطان الخلايا الكبدية (liver cell carcinoma), which is broadly related to مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ (jaundiced liver disease). This connection indicates that hepatocellular carcinoma often presents within the spectrum of liver diseases that cause jaundice, highlighting the importance of recognizing jaundice as a potential clinical clue to underlying malignant liver pathology. Understanding this relationship aids clinicians in early detection, accurate diagnosis, and appropriate management of patients with liver‑related malignancies and associated jaundice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 1288
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified within the broader category of neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn. This category also encompasses several distinct forms of neonatal jaundice. One form is neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation that results from the delayed development of the bilirubin‑conjugating system. Additional subtypes include breast‑milk‑induced jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي), jaundice that arises from immature hepatic conjugation in fetuses or newborns (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد), and jaundice that is associated with preterm birth (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان). Together, these conditions represent various manifestations of impaired bilirubin metabolism and hemolysis in the newborn period, highlighting the importance of recognizing specific etiologies for accurate diagnosis and appropriate management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
retrieved_docs.content: and perinatal jaundice are evaluated by total hemoglobin concentration, and laboratory values below reference ranges support their diagnosis. Neonatal jaundice manifests in multiple specific forms: neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, breast‑milk jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي), nuclear jaundice (يرقان نووي), nuclear jaundice caused by epidermal immaturity in the fetus or newborn (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد), physiologic neonatal jaundice (يرقان وليدي) and physiologic neonatal jaundice associated with preterm birth (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان). These jaundice types arise from hemolysis, delayed bilirubin conjugation, nutritional factors (breast‑milk) or birth‑timing complications, and they frequently co‑occur with hemorrhagic events. Hemorrhagic disorders anchored in the Neonatal period include loss of umbilical cord clamp leading to bleeding

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1096
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is categorized as a form of neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn. This disorder gives rise to several related manifestations of neonatal jaundice. It is specifically associated with neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, which reflects a delayed development of the hepatic conjugating system. The same clinical picture is described in Arabic as يرقان نووي and more precisely as يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد. Recognizing that these various terms all refer to the jaundice resulting from Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome and its broader category of neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease helps clinicians understand the full spectrum of presentations and guide appropriate patient care.
